properties.name: Barchan (Crescent) Dunes — Sam & Khuri, Jaisalmer
properties.source: Sikhwal Manchitrawali (16th ed.) + CAZRI + GSI publications

facts: Rajasthan's classic 'crescent' dunes — form perpendicular to the prevailing SW monsoon wind; migrate 3-15 m per year.
The Sam Sand Dunes and Khuri Sand Dunes (~42 km SW of Jaisalmer) are India's premier barchan tourism sites — the 'desert safari' venue.
Concave slip-face on the leeward side (~34° angle) and gentler stoss slope windward — the signature crescent profile that gave the type its name.

properties.name: Transverse Dunes — Bikaner Belt
properties.source: Sikhwal Manchitrawali (16th ed.) + CAZRI + GSI publications

facts: Long, wave-like ridges running perpendicular to the wind — form where sand supply is abundant and vegetation cover is low.
Bikaner-Ganganagar belt has Rajasthan's most extensive transverse dune fields — the IGNP command area was carved through this landscape.
Move slower than barchan (unified wave front stabilises the sand column); ridges 3-25 m tall running 100s of metres in length.

properties.name: Longitudinal (Seif) Dunes — Barmer
properties.source: Sikhwal Manchitrawali (16th ed.) + CAZRI + GSI publications

facts: Long parallel ridges running IN THE DIRECTION of the dominant wind — 'seif' = sword in Arabic, from the sword-shape profile.
The Barmer-Balotra belt hosts Rajasthan's longest longitudinal dunes — some ridges 15 km long, 20-30 m tall.
Form where two wind directions of nearly equal strength combine — the ridges align between the two vectors.

properties.name: Star Dunes — Shergarh, Jodhpur
properties.source: Sikhwal Manchitrawali (16th ed.) + CAZRI + GSI publications

facts: Pyramidal dunes with 3-5 arms radiating from a central peak — form where winds shift from multiple directions across the year.
The tallest dune type in the Thar (up to 100+ m); Shergarh-Phalodi belt has Rajasthan's best-developed star dunes.
Nearly stationary — each arm grows and retreats seasonally but the central peak barely moves; some star dunes here are dated to 4,000+ years old.
